prompt: You get ₦500 as pocket money. You want to buy a snack for ₦150. How much money will you have left?
hint: Take away the cost of the snack from your total money.
feedbackTip: Great job! Always know how much you have left after spending. This is called tracking your balance!

prompt: Your mum gives you ₦200 on Monday and ₦300 on Friday. You spend ₦150 on sweets during the week. How much do you have left to save?
hint: First add all the money you received, then subtract what you spent.
feedbackTip: You just did budgeting! Adding up money you receive and subtracting what you spend tells you your savings.

prompt: You earn ₦1,000 every week doing small chores at home. You want to buy a football that costs ₦3,500. How many weeks do you need to save ALL your money to buy it?
correctAnswer: 4 weeks
hint: Divide the price of the football by how much you earn per week.
feedbackTip: Saving up for something you want instead of borrowing money is called delayed gratification. It is a superpower!

prompt: You have ₦2,000. You want to save half of it and spend the rest. How much will you spend, and how much will you save?
correctAnswer: Save ₦1,000 and spend ₦1,000
hint: Half means dividing by 2.
feedbackTip: Saving half of what you receive is one of the best money habits you can build from a young age. Well done!

prompt: You earn ₦2,000 every week. You spend ₦500 on food, ₦300 on transport, and ₦200 on fun. How much is left? And what percentage of your ₦2,000 are you saving?
correctAnswer: ₦1,000 left, saving 50%
hint: Add up all your spending first. Then subtract from ₦2,000. To find the percentage, divide savings by total and multiply by 100.
feedbackTip: Financial experts say saving at least 20% of your income is great. You saved 50% — that is amazing! The 50-30-20 rule says: 50% needs, 30% wants, 20% savings.

prompt: You want to buy new school shoes that cost ₦8,000. Your weekly pocket money is ₦1,500 and you spend ₦800 on snacks and transport each week. Write a savings plan showing how many weeks it will take.
correctAnswer: Save ₦700 per week, need about 12 weeks
hint: First find how much you can save each week (pocket money minus spending). Then divide the shoe price by your weekly savings.
feedbackTip: You just made a savings plan! Real adults do this too. The key is knowing the difference between what you NEED to spend and what you WANT to spend.

prompt: A market woman has 48 oranges. She sells them in groups of 6. How many groups can she make?
hint: Divide 48 by 6.
feedbackTip: Division helps us share things equally. 48 ÷ 6 = 8. You can use this skill every day at the market!

prompt: Tunde buys 3 pens at ₦75 each. How much does he pay in total?
hint: Multiply 3 by 75.
feedbackTip: Multiplication is just fast addition! 75 + 75 + 75 = 225. Knowing this stops people from overcharging you at the market.

prompt: A bus leaves Lagos at 7:30am and arrives in Ibadan at 10:15am. How long was the journey?
correctAnswer: 2 hours 45 mins
hint: Count from 7:30 to 10:15. First count the hours, then the minutes.
feedbackTip: Time calculations help you plan your day. From 7:30 to 10:30 is 3 hours. But 10:15 is 15 minutes before 10:30, so subtract 15 minutes. That gives 2 hours 45 minutes!

prompt: In a class of 40 students, 25 are girls. What percentage of the class are boys?
hint: First find the number of boys. Then divide by total students and multiply by 100.
feedbackTip: Boys = 40 - 25 = 15. Percentage = (15 ÷ 40) × 100 = 37.5%. Percentages help us compare numbers easily!

prompt: Amaka sells puff puff at ₦50 each. She makes 60 puff puffs and sells 45 of them. How much money did she make, and what percentage of her puff puff did she NOT sell?
correctAnswer: She made ₦2,250. She did not sell 25% of her puff puff.
hint: Money = 45 × ₦50. Unsold = 60 - 45 = 15. Percentage unsold = (15 ÷ 60) × 100.
feedbackTip: You just calculated profit and waste percentage — skills real business owners use every day! Reducing unsold stock saves money.

prompt: A phone costs ₦45,000. A shop is offering a 20% discount. What is the new price? If you only have ₦30,000, how much more do you need?
correctAnswer: Discount is ₦9,000. New price is ₦36,000. You need ₦6,000 more.
hint: 20% of ₦45,000 = (20 ÷ 100) × 45,000. Subtract that from ₦45,000 to get the new price.
feedbackTip: Discounts save you money — but only if you actually need the item! Always calculate the real price before getting excited about a sale.

prompt: Your teacher asks you to introduce yourself to a new student. Write 3 sentences you would say.
correctAnswer: Any confident, friendly 3-sentence introduction
hint: Include your name, something you like, and a welcome.
feedbackTip: A great introduction has 3 things: your name, something interesting about you, and warmth. First impressions matter — practice this until it feels natural!

prompt: Your friend says something that hurt your feelings. Write what you would say to them — be honest but kind.
correctAnswer: Any response that expresses feelings without being aggressive
hint: Start with 'I feel...' instead of 'You always...'
feedbackTip: Starting sentences with 'I feel...' instead of blaming the other person is called using an I-statement. It helps people hear you without getting defensive.

prompt: Your school wants to cancel the end-of-year party. You disagree. Write 3 strong reasons why the party should happen.
correctAnswer: Any 3 well-reasoned, respectful arguments
hint: Think about the benefits of the party for students. Each reason should be different.
feedbackTip: Good arguments use evidence and think about the other person's concerns. Always be respectful — the strongest speakers stay calm even when they disagree.

prompt: You are asked to give a 1-minute speech about why reading is important. Write out what you would say from start to finish.
correctAnswer: Any structured short speech with opening, points, and closing
hint: Open with something that grabs attention, give 2 reasons, then close strongly.
feedbackTip: Every great speech has 3 parts: a strong opening to grab attention, your main points, and a memorable closing. Even 1 minute is enough to change someone's mind!

prompt: Your younger sibling wants to drop out of school to sell things on the street. Write what you would say to convince them to stay in school. Make your argument as strong as possible.
correctAnswer: Any empathetic, persuasive argument that acknowledges their feelings and offers solid reasons
hint: First understand WHY they want to leave. Then show them a better path. Don't lecture — connect.
feedbackTip: The most powerful persuasion starts with understanding. When you show someone you understand their feelings first, they are much more likely to listen to your argument.

prompt: You are 13 years old and you want to start a small business selling zobo drink at school. Write a short pitch to convince your parents to support you.
correctAnswer: Any confident, well-reasoned pitch with a plan and response to concerns
hint: Tell them your plan, how you will handle school work, and how much money you could make. Answer the worries they will definitely have.
feedbackTip: A great pitch answers questions before they are asked. Think about what the other person is worried about and address it directly. This is called handling objections!

prompt: NEPA took the light and your torch battery is dead. You need to read for a test tomorrow. List 3 creative ways you could get light.
correctAnswer: Any 3 practical creative solutions e.g. candle, phone screen, go to a neighbour's
hint: Think about everything around you in the house. What gives light?
feedbackTip: Creativity is not magic — it is looking at what you already have and using it in a new way. This is called resourcefulness, and it is one of the most valuable life skills!

prompt: Your school is having a competition for the best handmade gift for a teacher. You have only paper, a pen, and scissors. What would you make and why?
correctAnswer: Any thoughtful creative idea using the given materials
hint: Think about what would make your teacher feel special. It does not have to be expensive to be meaningful.
feedbackTip: The best creative solutions work within limits. Some of the world's greatest inventions came from people who had very little. Constraints make you more creative, not less!

prompt: Your school has no library but students love reading. You have an empty classroom after school hours. Design a simple reading club using only things you already have at school.
correctAnswer: Any organised, practical reading club plan using existing resources
hint: Think about books (where will they come from?), space, timing, and how to make it fun.
feedbackTip: Solving real problems with limited resources is called frugal innovation. Many of Nigeria's most successful entrepreneurs started by solving everyday problems with what they had around them.

prompt: You notice that many kids in your neighbourhood throw pure water sachets on the ground. Come up with a creative idea to solve this problem using things kids already have.
correctAnswer: Any creative, practical environmental solution
hint: Think about what would motivate kids to change their behaviour. Games? Rewards? Friendly competition?
feedbackTip: The best solutions change behaviour by making the right choice feel fun or rewarding. This is called a nudge — and it is used by governments and companies worldwide!

prompt: Imagine you have ₦5,000 and one week. Design a small business idea you could start in your neighbourhood. Explain what you would sell, who your customers are, and how you would make a profit.
correctAnswer: Any well-thought-out, realistic small business plan with product, customers, and profit logic
hint: Think about problems in your area that people would pay someone to solve. What do your neighbours need?
feedbackTip: Every big business started as a small idea. The best businesses solve a real problem for real people. You just thought like an entrepreneur!

prompt: Your class needs to raise ₦20,000 for a school trip in 3 weeks. You cannot ask parents for money. Come up with a fundraising plan.
correctAnswer: Any practical, ethical fundraising plan with activities and realistic income estimates
hint: Think about what skills your classmates have that people would pay for. Food? Cleaning? Performances?
feedbackTip: Fundraising is creative problem-solving with a money target. Breaking the big goal into small daily actions makes it achievable. ₦20,000 over 3 weeks is less than ₦1,000 per day!

prompt: Your best friend got a higher score than you in an exam and is very excited. You feel a little jealous. What do you do?
options: Congratulate them and ask how they studied
Tell them their score is not that impressive
Pretend you are happy but feel bad inside
correctAnswer: Congratulate them and ask how they studied
hint: Think about what a good friend would do — and also what would benefit YOU.
feedbackTip: Jealousy is normal — everyone feels it. What matters is what you do with it. Turning jealousy into curiosity ('how did they do that?') is a superpower!

prompt: You are very angry at your classmate who accidentally spilled water on your book. Describe what you would do BEFORE you say or do anything.
correctAnswer: Any response involving pausing, breathing, or calming down before reacting
hint: The first few seconds after something bad happens are the most dangerous. What can you do in those seconds?
feedbackTip: Taking 3 deep breaths before reacting gives your brain time to think instead of just react. Scientists call this the pause-and-plan response. It prevents most arguments!

prompt: Your classmate has been very quiet and sad all week. They say 'I am fine' when you ask. What do you do to show you genuinely care without forcing them to talk?
correctAnswer: Any response that shows care without pressure e.g. staying close, small gestures, checking in gently
hint: Sometimes people are not ready to talk. How can you show you care without pushing?
feedbackTip: Real empathy means being present without pressure. Sometimes just saying 'I am here if you need me' and following through is worth more than any advice.

prompt: Two of your friends are having an argument and both come to you for support. Each one says the other is wrong. What do you do?
correctAnswer: Any neutral, fair response that listens to both sides without taking sides or spreading gossip
hint: You do not have to choose a side. What is the most helpful thing you can do for both of them?
feedbackTip: Being a fair listener without gossiping or taking sides builds deep trust. People remember who stayed calm and fair when things got difficult.

prompt: You worked really hard on a group project but your groupmates did very little. The teacher praised the whole group equally. You feel it is unfair. How do you handle this without causing trouble in the group?
correctAnswer: Any mature response that addresses feelings, communicates assertively, and keeps relationships intact
hint: Think about what outcome you actually want. Do you want to be right, or do you want things to improve?
feedbackTip: Assertiveness means expressing how you feel clearly and calmly — not aggressively, not silently. It is one of the hardest and most important communication skills to master.

prompt: A new student joins your class and is being laughed at by others because of how they speak. Nobody is defending them. What do you do, and why is it difficult?
correctAnswer: Any response that shows courage to defend, acknowledges social risk, and explains the right values
hint: Think honestly about what makes it hard to speak up. Then think about what kind of person you want to be.
feedbackTip: Standing up for someone when it is socially risky is called moral courage. It is rare and powerful. The people who do it are remembered — by the ones they helped, and by everyone watching.
